Client 2: No, it’s higher than that—closer to, what, 190?
Advisor: Got it—so not net take-home, I mean gross before taxes. Do you want to pin it down to a single number you’re comfortable with?
Client 1: Gross, okay. Combined gross is about $187,750 a year.
Advisor: Perfect—$187,750 gross household. And just so I’m tracking sources: Michael, you’ve got income coming in monthly, and Mary, you’ve got a pension coming in monthly—does that sound right?
Client 2: Yes, mine’s a pension, monthly.

Advisor: Great. Going back to what you said, Mary, about not guessing every month—let’s talk spending. About how much do you spend per month all-in, like household expenses?
Client 2: I think around $7,000 a month?
Client 1: It’s more than that—sorry—closer to $7,700.
Advisor: Okay, $7,700 a month in total expenses. When you say expenses, are you including debt payments in that, or is that separate in your mind?
Client 2: I… I include the mortgage, yeah. Isn’t that just an expense?

Client 1: Mortgage is $1,550 a month.
Client 2: And there’s that little loan thing—like $150 a month.
Advisor: So debt payments together would be about $1,700 per month, right?
Client 2: Yes, about $1,700.
Client 1: Right.

Client 2: I thought it was more like mid-seven hundreds.
Advisor: Let’s tighten it up. If you had to give me the best estimate today—what number should I write down for investable assets total?
Client 1: Write down $736,550.
Advisor: Great—$736,550 investable. And I’ll ask a couple quick buckets so I’m not missing anything: do you have a joint taxable brokerage account?
Client 1: Yes—joint brokerage, taxable.

Client 1: Yeah, I’ve got a private markets thing through an insurance company.
Advisor: Okay. And then on the property side, you’ve got your primary residence—do you have a sense of the home value today?
Client 2: Around $273,500.
Advisor: Great. And the mortgage balance outstanding—about $140k still?
Client 1: $140,450, roughly.

Advisor: Okay. And then on the property side, you’ve got your primary residence—do you have a sense of the home value today?
Client 2: Around $273,500.
Advisor: Great. And the mortgage balance outstanding—about $140k still?
Client 1: $140,450, roughly.
Advisor: Perfect. And just to make sure I didn’t miss protection—life insurance, disability, long-term care—anything in place right now?

Advisor: Okay. And then on the property side, you’ve got your primary residence—do you have a sense of the home value today?
Client 2: Around $273,500.
Advisor: Great. And the mortgage balance outstanding—about $140k still?
Client 1: $140,450, roughly.
Advisor: Perfect. And just to make sure I didn’t miss protection—life insurance, disability, long-term care—anything in place right now?

Advisor: We’ve got the mortgage at $140,450. Besides that, do you have any other loans or balances we should count—like a small personal loan, card, anything like that?
Client 2: Just that little one, the high-interest one we mentioned—like six hundred-ish.
Client 1: It’s $650, not like two grand or anything.
Advisor: Got it—$650 outstanding on the non-mortgage loan. And that one’s the 21.95% interest, right?
Client 2: Yes—well, I mean, that’s the rate, not the payment.

Client 1: Yeah, seriously. Anyway—go ahead.
Advisor: Alright—thanks. Going back to what you said earlier about taxes making you a little uneasy, Michael—what bracket do you usually think of yourselves in these days?
Client 1: We’re in the 35% bracket, pretty consistently.
Client 2: I mean—yeah, 35%. It feels like everything lands there.
Advisor: Got it, 35%. And just so I’m not mixing terms—when you say 35%, you mean your federal tax bracket, not like your effective rate, right?

Advisor: Hi Michael, hi Mary—good to meet you both. I’m Alex. Before we dive in, how’s your day going so far?
Client 2: Oh, busy. Traffic was a mess, but we made it.
Client 1: Yeah, we’re fine—just trying to keep this tight.
Advisor: Totally hear you. We’ll keep it moving. Michael, Mary—tell me what prompted you to come in now.

Advisor: Hi Michael, hi Mary—good to meet you both. I’m Alex. Before we dive in, how’s your day going so far?
Client 2: Oh, busy. Traffic was a mess, but we made it.
Client 1: Yeah, we’re fine—just trying to keep this tight.
Advisor: Totally hear you. We’ll keep it moving. Michael, Mary—tell me what prompted you to come in now.

Client 2: Yes, two adults.
Client 1: Yep, just us—two adults.
Advisor: Perfect. And you two are married, correct?
Client 2: Yes, we’re married.
Client 1: Married, yeah.

Client 2: And I want to know we can actually live on what we have without guessing every month.
Advisor: Okay. Let’s start with the big picture income, then we’ll go expenses, then assets and debts. Roughly, what’s your combined gross household income in a year?
Client 1: Ballpark, like 180-ish?
Client 2: No, it’s higher than that—closer to, what, 190?
Advisor: Got it—so not net take-home, I mean gross before taxes. Do you want to pin it down to a single number you’re comfortable with?

Client 1: Yeah, zero dependents at this point.
Advisor: Perfect. And where are you two primarily resident—what state are you filing from these days?
Client 1: Florida.
Client 2: Yeah, FL—still Florida.
Advisor: Got it. I always ask because it affects taxes and planning assumptions. And just so I’m not mixing terms—when you say Florida, you mean your primary residence is in Florida, not like a second place you spend winters at, right?

Client 2: No, we’re good. It’s been raining here, but the connection’s okay.
Client 1: Yeah, fine on our end.
Advisor: Great. Switching gears for one second—when we work together, we’re in what’s called the US RIA channel. Have you heard “RIA” before?
Client 2: Is that like… a bank advisor?
Advisor: Close—good question. It’s not a bank; it’s a Registered Investment Advisor in the US. So, US RIA—does that match what you were expecting from this relationship?

Client 1: Correct—I'm Client 1.
Client 2: And I’m Client 2, yes.
Advisor: Great. Now, earlier you said you’re moderate on risk—before I lock that in, tell me what “moderate” means to you, Michael. Like, are we talking normal ups and downs are fine, but no big swings?
Client 1: Yeah, moderate. I can handle market movement, I just don’t want anything that puts the business cash at risk.
Client 2: I’m a little more conservative than he is, honestly.

Advisor: Okay, about $107,900-ish gross annually—close enough.
Client 1: Yeah, $107,900 is the number I’ve got in my head.
Advisor: And Mary, your gross annual income from work is about $79,900, right?
Client 2: Yes—about $79,900.
Advisor: Now, earlier we also had two monthly income lines on the sheet—this is where it can get confusing. Michael, do you have a monthly salary draw that annualizes to about $66,750?

Advisor: Okay—one sec, I’m just writing that down.
Advisor: Since you wanted to keep moving, I’ll keep this tight. We already hit assets and debts, but I want to make sure we’ve touched each bucket cleanly.
Advisor: Income first—Michael, earlier you mentioned your income is kind of lumpy with the practice. If you had to put a gross annual number on your self-employed income, what’s the ballpark?
Client 1: Roughly $108,000 gross a year.
Advisor: Okay, about $107,900-ish gross annually—close enough.

Client 2: Yes, $79,900 gross.
Advisor: Great. And I know we’ve been bouncing around—thanks for sticking with me. Mary, can I grab your date of birth as well?
Client 2: Yeah, it’s December 4th, 1966.
Advisor: December 4, 1966—thank you. Michael, you’re good with me capturing that for Mary?
Client 1: Yeah, that’s right.

Advisor: Yeah, the rain’s been nonstop lately. Okay—going back to the people details for a minute, Mary, I want to make sure I’ve got your role and basics right.
Client 2: Sure.
Advisor: In the household, you’re Michael’s spouse/partner, correct?
Client 2: Yes, I’m the spouse.
Client 1: Yep—Mary’s my spouse.

Client 1: And I’m Client 2. It’s backwards from what we said—my bad.
Advisor: Okay, thank you—so Mary is Client 1 and Michael is Client 2. I’ll stick with that going forward.
Advisor: Mary, one more about your work—when you say W-2 employed, what kind of role is it? Like professional, admin, healthcare—how would you bucket it?
Client 2: Professional. Office job, but yeah—professional.
Advisor: Great, professional—thank you.
